critique.summary: The article provides a detailed overview of the new features and changes introduced in Java 26, including updates to the HotSpot JVM, HTTP client API with HTTP/3 support, structured concurrency, lazy constants, and enhancements to pattern matching with primitive types. It also covers deprecations and preparations for future Java improvements, particularly related to Project Valhalla.
mainPoints: Java 26 introduces new HotSpot JVM features such as ahead-of-time object caching compatible with any garbage collector and improvements to G1 garbage collector throughput.
The HTTP client API now supports HTTP/3 with multiple negotiation strategies to handle varying server capabilities.
Structured concurrency is enhanced to provide better task hierarchy, cancellation, and error propagation in concurrent programming.
Lazy constants are introduced to allow deferred and thread-safe initialization of immutable objects, improving startup performance and concurrency safety.
Pattern matching is extended to support primitive types in instanceof and switch constructs, improving type safety and expressiveness.
Several features are in preview or incubator stages, requiring enabling preview flags for use.
Java 26 prepares to restrict mutation of final fields via deep reflection to improve program correctness and JVM optimizations.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately summarizes the official JEPs (JDK Enhancement Proposals) and features introduced in Java 26 as documented by the OpenJDK project and Oracle. The descriptions of JEPs such as 516, 517, 522, 524, 525, 526, 529, and 530 align with publicly available information from official Java release notes and JEP documentation. The explanations of technical details, use cases, and preview statuses are consistent with authoritative sources. The article does not cite multiple external sources but is consistent with known official Java development communications.
considerations: The article does not provide multiple perspectives or external expert commentary, which are typically required for a fully reliable journalistic piece.
Some features are in preview or incubator stages, meaning they are experimental and subject to change in future Java releases.
The impact of these features on existing Java applications and ecosystems is not deeply discussed.
The article assumes reader familiarity with Java internals and terminology, which may limit accessibility for general audiences.
No discussion is provided about potential drawbacks or challenges in adopting new features like HTTP/3 or structured concurrency.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion reflects a mix of nostalgia, appreciation, and criticism regarding Java's evolution, ecosystem, and culture. Several commenters highlight Java's improvements and modern features, while others express frustration with its legacy and ecosystem challenges, especially in relation to Android and cultural resistance.

comments.author: ludovicianul
comments.text: I program in Java for more than 15 years now. I can resonate with people hating the language from it's early days due to the experience with all the enterprisy features and over abstractions. Or confunding Java with the Spring ecosystem. But Java came a long way over the years. It's now what many would call a "modern" language. It's less verbose, has many of the features people find appealing in Scala and Kotlin and it can even compile to native binaries using GraalVM. This made building CLIs in Java feasible. Or lambdas.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced and informed perspective on Java's evolution, highlighting concrete improvements and modern features, making it highly accurate and constructive.

comments.author: einrealist
comments.text: I have been using Java since version 1.4. Both the language and its ecosystem have come a long way since then. I endured the height of the EJB phase. I adopted Spring when version 1.2 was released. I spent hours fighting with IDEs to run OSGi bundles. I hated building UIs with Swing/AWT, many of which are still in use today and are gradually being replaced by lovely JavaFX. When I look at code I wrote around 12 years ago, I'm amazed at how much I've matured too.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a personal historical overview of Java's ecosystem, reflecting accurate experiences and acknowledging progress over time.

comments.author: xeubie
comments.text:
I think astronomers could measure the age of the universe in nano-Valhallas. Every year, it feels 50% closer to completion...

In all seriousness I'm happy with what Mr. Goetz and the team have done. Sealed interfaces (java 17) + exhaustive switch statements (java 21) means we now have union types in java! And instead of jumping on the async/await bandwagon we now have a more general solution that doesn't lead to API duplication (virtual threads). But Valhalla has been a veeery long time coming.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately describes recent Java language features and improvements, demonstrating good technical understanding.

comments.author: dzonga
comments.text:
the people that work on Java & the JVM are very smart.

it has become a best of breed language - hell its better than Go for industry purposes.

the drawback with Java will always be the CULTURE - (maybe someone can insert a quote of how in physics progress is only made, when old physicist die - I don't wanna be morbid ) but with Java same that's when the culture will change.

All those people using typescript (could be using Java - but the culture doesn't want them and consider them heretics for not embracing religion of OOP and FactoryFactory)
comments.analysis: While somewhat emotional, the comment insightfully points out cultural resistance as a major factor in Java's ecosystem challenges, which is a well-recognized issue.

comments.author: vyskocilm
comments.text:
JEP 504: Remove the Applet API

Glad to see this being removed. Java plugins especially on Linux were awful and required by tons of corporate stuff. Anyone remeber IcedTea Web? A functional and opensource Java plugin and Java Webstart implementation?
comments.analysis: This comment accurately notes the removal of outdated and problematic Java features, reflecting a positive development in the language's evolution.

comments.author: freedomben
comments.text: As someone who has been out of the Java world for many years, but recently forced back into it due to Android dev requirements, Post lawsuit, what is the relationship between Android (Google) and Java now?  When can we expect 26 on Android?  On that note, why is Android always so far behind?  Is it because Kotlin is primary or is it deeper?  Did the lawsuit play a role?
comments.analysis: This comment raises relevant and accurate questions about Android's Java support and ecosystem delays, reflecting common concerns among developers.

comments.author: wiseowise
comments.text: Android as always in shambles. Shame that neither Google, nor ecosystem cares about Java because they’ve bought JetBrains kopium.
comments.analysis: The comment is emotionally charged but reflects a widely held view about Android's prioritization of Kotlin over Java and the resulting ecosystem fragmentation.

comments.author: Bnjoroge
comments.text: Man I still have scars learning Java in college. Took the entire fun out of coding until I ended up learning C.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses a personal negative experience with Java, which is subjective but common among some learners due to Java's verbosity and complexity.

comments.author: oystersareyum
comments.text: Do changes to Java itself impact e.g. Clojure? I think it uses many java primitives instead of emitting jvm byte code but I can be wrong.
comments.analysis: The question is valid; Clojure runs on the JVM and is affected by JVM changes, though it compiles to JVM bytecode. The comment shows curiosity but slight uncertainty.

comments.author: dxxvi
comments.text: Does the Java team use AI? If not, they should use it to give us more features and better performance in a release.
comments.analysis: While constructive, this comment speculates without evidence about AI usage in Java development and oversimplifies complex development processes.

comments.author: rusakov-field
comments.text: Ah , Java, a language I pray I never have to ever touch again.
comments.analysis: This comment is purely emotional and adds no substantive insight or constructive feedback to the discussion.
